from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of building‑related triples into a fluent, natural‑language sentence.
    """
    # Mapping from predicate to a readable phrase (more concise for common predicates)
    verb_map = {
        "architecturalStyle": "has an architectural style of",
        "buildingStartDate": "was built in",
        "completionDate": "was completed in",
        "floorCount": "has",
        "location": "is located in",
        "cost": "costs",
        "floorArea": "covers a floor area of",
        "architect": "was designed by",
        "owner": "is owned by",
        "formerName": "was formerly known as",
        "height": "rises to",
        "buildingType": "is a",
        "developer": "was developed by",
        "tenant": "has a tenant",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "country": "is in",
        "currentTenants": "currently houses",
        "address": "has the address",
        "inaugurationDate": "was inaugurated on",
        "birthPlace": "was born in",
        "deathPlace": "died in",
        "bedCount": "has",
        "region": "is in the region of",
        "state": "is in the state of",
        "website": "has website",
        "yearOfConstruction": "was constructed in",
        "NationalRegisterOfHistoricPlacesReferenceNumber": "has NRHP reference number",
        "addedToTheNationalRegisterOfHistoricPlaces": "was added to the NRHP on",
        "significantBuilding": "is known for the building",
        "governingBody": "is governed by",
        "leader": "is led by",
        "chancellor": "has chancellor",
        "governmentType": "has government type",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "language": "has language",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "currency": "uses currency",
        "ethnicGroup": "has ethnic group",
        "origin": "originates from",
        "significantProject": "is known for the project",
        "foundationPlace": "was founded in",
        "demonym": "has demonym",
        "numberOfRooms": "has",
        "keyPerson": "has key person",
        "architecture": "features architecture",
        "postalCode": "has the postcode area",
    }

    # Noun suffixes for count‑type predicates
    count_noun_map = {
        "floorCount": "floors",
        "bedCount": "beds",
        "numberOfRooms": "rooms",
    }

    # If there is exactly one triple, use a more natural template.
    if len(triples) == 1:
        t = triples[0]
        template_map = {
            "demonym": "The demonym of {subject} is {object}.",
            "leader": "The leader of {subject} is {object}.",
            "capital": "The capital of {subject} is {object}.",
            "location": "The {subject} is located at {object}.",
            "height": "{subject} rises to {object}.",
            "architect": "{subject} was designed by {object}.",
            "owner": "{subject} is owned by {object}.",
            "buildingStartDate": "Construction of {subject} started in {object}.",
            "completionDate": "{subject} was completed in {object}.",
            "floorCount": "{subject} has {object} floors.",
            "floorArea": "{subject} covers a floor area of {object} square metres.",
            "cost": "{subject} cost {object}.",
            "buildingType": "{subject} is a {object}.",
            "country": "{subject} is in {object}.",
            "isPartOf": "{subject} is part of {object}.",
            "formerName": "{subject} was formerly known as {object}.",
            "tenant": "{subject} has a tenant {object}.",
            "developer": "{subject} was developed by {object}.",
            "currentTenants": "{subject} currently houses {object}.",
            "address": "{subject} has the address {object}.",
            "inaugurationDate": "{subject} was inaugurated on {object}.",
            "birthPlace": "{subject} was born in {object}.",
            "deathPlace": "{subject} died in {object}.",
            "bedCount": "{subject} has {object} beds.",
            "region": "{subject} is in the region of {object}.",
            "state": "{subject} is in the state of {object}.",
            "website": "{subject} has website {object}.",
            "yearOfConstruction": "{subject} was constructed in {object}.",
            "NationalRegisterOfHistoricPlacesReferenceNumber": "{subject} has NRHP reference number {object}.",
            "addedToTheNationalRegisterOfHistoricPlaces": "{subject} was added to the NRHP on {object}.",
            "significantBuilding": "{subject} is known for the building {object}.",
            "governingBody": "{subject} is governed by {object}.",
            "chancellor": "{subject} has chancellor {object}.",
            "governmentType": "{subject} has government type {object}.",
            "language": "{subject} has language {object}.",
            "leaderTitle": "{subject} has leader title {object}.",
            "currency": "{subject} uses currency {object}.",
            "ethnicGroup": "{subject} has ethnic group {object}.",
            "origin": "{subject} originates from {object}.",
            "significantProject": "{subject} is known for the project {object}.",
            "foundationPlace": "{subject} was founded in {object}.",
            "numberOfRooms": "{subject} has {object} rooms.",
            "keyPerson": "{subject} has key person {object}.",
            "architecture": "{subject} features architecture {object}.",
            "postalCode": "{subject} has the postcode area {object}.",
        }
        tmpl = template_map.get(t.predicate)
        if tmpl:
            return tmpl.format(subject=t.subject, object=t.object).strip()
        # Fallback to generic phrasing
        verb = verb_map.get(t.predicate, "has")
        return f"{t.subject} {verb} {t.object}."

    # Group triples by their subject to avoid repeating the subject in every clause.
    from collections import defaultdict
    subject_groups = defaultdict(list)
    for t in triples:
        subject_groups[t.subject].append(t)

    sentences = []
    for subject, group in subject_groups.items():
        parts = []

        # Preferred ordering of common attributes for readability.
        order = ["location", "height", "architect", "buildingType", "completionDate"]
        handled = set()

        # Helper to add a clause for a given predicate if present.
        def add_clause(pred):
            for tr in group:
                if tr.predicate == pred:
                    verb = verb_map.get(pred, "has")
                    # Special phrasing for some predicates
                    if pred == "height":
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    elif pred == "location":
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    else:
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    parts.append(clause)
                    handled.add(pred)
                    break

        for pred in order:
            add_clause(pred)

        # Add remaining predicates that were not handled above.
        for tr in group:
            if tr.predicate in handled:
                continue
            verb = verb_map.get(tr.predicate, "has")
            # Count‑type predicates need a noun.
            if tr.predicate in count_noun_map:
                noun = count_noun_map[tr.predicate]
                clause = f"{verb} {tr.object} {noun}"
            else:
                clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
            parts.append(clause)

        # Build the sentence for this subject.
        sentence = f"{subject} " + ", ".join(parts) + "."
        sentences.append(sentence)

    # Combine sentences for multiple subjects.
    final_text = " ".join(sentences)
    # Capitalize the first character of the whole output.
    if final_text:
        final_text = final_text[0].upper() + final_text[1:]
    return final_text

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

You will be given the current Python program with its score and your task will be to evolve the current Python program to improve the score. It is a program that converts a list of triples into natural language text. The given triples will be associated with the topic: Building. The program must implement a function called 'predict' that accepts a list of triples and generates a coherent, contextually relevant sentence that accurately represents the information contained in the triples. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. Converting all those triples into one sentence will award greater score. An example of a complex sentence:
Triples:
(Antwerp | cityServed | Antwerp International Airport)
(Belgium | country | Antwerp)
(City of Brussels | capital | Belgium)
Example sentence:
"Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

@dataclass
class Triple:

    """

        "origin": "originates from",

        "foundationPlace": "was founded in",
        "demonym": "has demonym",
        "numberOfRooms": "has",
        "keyPerson": "has key person",
        "architecture": "features architecture",
        "postalCode": "has the postcode area",
    }

    # Noun suffixes for count‑type predicates
    count_noun_map = {
        "floorCount": "floors",
        "bedCount": "beds",
        "numberOfRooms": "rooms",
    }

    # Group triples by their subject to avoid repeating the subject in every clause.
    from collections import defaultdict
    subject_groups = defaultdict(list)
    for t in triples:
        subject_groups[t.subject].append(t)

    sentences = []
    for subject, group in subject_groups.items():
        parts = []

        # Preferred ordering of common attributes for readability.
        order = ["location", "height", "architect", "buildingType", "completionDate"]
        handled = set()

        # Helper to add a clause for a given predicate if present.
        def add_clause(pred):
            for tr in group:
                if tr.predicate == pred:
                    verb = verb_map.get(pred, "has")
                    # Special phrasing for some predicates
                    if pred == "height":
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    elif pred == "location":
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    else:
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    parts.append(clause)
                    handled.add(pred)
                    break

        for pred in order:
            add_clause(pred)

        # Add remaining predicates that were not handled above.
        for tr in group:
            if tr.predicate in handled:
                continue
            verb = verb_map.get(tr.predicate, "has")
            # Count‑type predicates need a noun.
            if tr.predicate in count_noun_map:
                noun = count_noun_map[tr.predicate]
                clause = f"{verb} {tr.object} {noun}"
            else:
                clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
            parts.append(clause)

        # Build the sentence for this subject.
        sentence = f"{subject} " + ", ".join(parts) + "."
        sentences.append(sentence)

    # Combine sentences for multiple subjects.
    final_text = " ".join(sentences)
    # Capitalize the first character of the whole output.
    if final_text:
        final_text = final_text[0].upper() + final_text[1:]
    return final_text

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

            "foundationPlace": "{subject} was founded in {object}.",
            "numberOfRooms": "{subject} has {object} rooms.",
            "keyPerson": "{subject} has key person {object}.",
            "architecture": "{subject} features architecture {object}.",
            "postalCode": "{subject} has the postcode area {object}.",
        }
        tmpl = template_map.get(t.predicate)
        if tmpl:
            return tmpl.format(subject=t.subject, object=t.object).strip()
        # Fallback to generic phrasing
        verb = verb_map.get(t.predicate, "has")
        return f"{t.subject} {verb} {t.object}."

    # Group triples by their subject to avoid repeating the subject in every clause.
    from collections import defaultdict
    subject_groups = defaultdict(list)
    for t in triples:
        subject_groups[t.subject].append(t)

    sentences = []
    for subject, group in subject_groups.items():
        parts = []

        # Preferred ordering of common attributes for readability.
        order = ["location", "height", "architect", "buildingType", "completionDate"]
        handled = set()

        # Helper to add a clause for a given predicate if present.
        def add_clause(pred):
            for tr in group:
                if tr.predicate == pred:
                    verb = verb_map.get(pred, "has")
                    # Special phrasing for some predicates
                    if pred == "height":
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    elif pred == "location":
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    else:
                        clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
                    parts.append(clause)
                    handled.add(pred)
                    break

        for pred in order:
            add_clause(pred)

        # Add remaining predicates that were not handled above.
        for tr in group:
            if tr.predicate in handled:
                continue
            verb = verb_map.get(tr.predicate, "has")
            # Count‑type predicates need a noun.
            if tr.predicate in count_noun_map:
                noun = count_noun_map[tr.predicate]
                clause = f"{verb} {tr.object} {noun}"
            else:
                clause = f"{verb} {tr.object}"
            parts.append(clause)

        # Build the sentence for this subject.
        sentence = f"{subject} " + ", ".join(parts) + "."
        sentences.append(sentence)

    # Combine sentences for multiple subjects.
    final_text = " ".join(sentences)
    # Capitalize the first character of the whole output.
    if final_text:
        final_text = final_text[0].upper() + final_text[1:]
    return final_text
